title: United States v. Aaron Swartz — Superseding Indictment (Crim. No. 11-CR-10260, D. Mass., Sept. 12, 2012)
organization: U.S. District Court for the District of Massachusetts (filed by the U.S. Attorney's Office)
bias_or_interest: The prosecution's own charging document. Authoritative for the exact counts charged (the 13-felony superseding indictment) and the statutes invoked (CFAA and wire fraud).
relevant_locations: Thirteen felony counts (superseding indictment, Sept 2012)
Charges under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (18 U.S.C. 1030) and wire fraud (18 U.S.C. 1343)
Allegation: ~4.8 million JSTOR articles downloaded over MIT's network
quotation_note: Use for the count numbers and statutes only. State the maximum sentence as the THEORETICAL stacked statutory maximum, never as a realistic outcome.
notes: The original July 2011 indictment had 4 counts; this 2012 superseding indictment raised it to 13. Durable hash to be computed before publish.

title: Alleged Hacker Charged With Stealing Over Four Million Documents from MIT Network (U.S. Attorney, District of Massachusetts, press release)
organization: U.S. Department of Justice — U.S. Attorney's Office, District of Massachusetts
bias_or_interest: The prosecutor's own announcement of the original charges. Source of U.S. Attorney Carmen Ortiz's 'Stealing is stealing...' statement.
relevant_locations: Original four-count indictment (July 2011)
Carmen Ortiz quote: 'Stealing is stealing whether you use a computer command or a crowbar...'
Allegation of ~4.8 million articles from JSTOR via MIT's network
quotation_note: Quote Ortiz's 'Stealing is stealing' line as the prosecutor's attributed position, not as the narrator's view.
notes: Primary for the prosecution framing and the original count number.

title: Report to the President: MIT and the Prosecution of Aaron Swartz (the 'Abelson Report')
author: Hal Abelson, Peter A. Diamond, Andrew Grosso, Douglas W. Pfeiffer (review panel)
bias_or_interest: MIT's own commissioned review. Found no MIT wrongdoing but criticized MIT's hands-off, 'neutral' posture as a missed opportunity. Institutional self-examination, so weigh its self-interest.
relevant_locations: MIT took a position of neutrality; was not affiliated with Swartz
Finding: no MIT misconduct, but MIT 'missed an opportunity' to lead
Swartz's father publicly rejected the 'neutral' characterization
quotation_note: Attribute the 'neutral / missed an opportunity' findings to the report; attribute the rejection of 'neutral' to his father.
notes: Key for the MIT neutrality question and the systemic critique. Verify exact wording before publish.

title: Official Statement from the Family and Partner of Aaron Swartz
author: The family and partner of Aaron Swartz
bias_or_interest: The family's own statement, written in grief. The single authorized source for the family's view that prosecutorial overreach contributed to his death and that MIT 'refused to stand up.' Plainly interested; must be attributed, never adopted as the narrator's factual conclusion.
relevant_locations: Family statement: decisions of the prosecutors and MIT contributed to his death
'a crime with no victims'
MIT 'refused to stand up'
quotation_note: ALWAYS attribute single-cause language about his death to the family's statement. The narrator never asserts a cause as fact.
rights_note: Quote briefly, attributed; do not reproduce at length.
notes: The mandatory attribution anchor for the death/causation lock.

title: Prosecutor as Bully (essay by Lawrence Lessig)
author: Lawrence Lessig
bias_or_interest: A close friend and mentor; legal scholar. Strong, named critique of the prosecution's proportionality. Clearly a partisan voice for Swartz; attribute as opinion.
relevant_locations: Lessig: the question is whether the government's behavior was proportional
'bully' framing of the prosecution
quotation_note: Attribute to Lessig as named opinion; do not present as established fact.
notes: Names a credible critic for the proportionality argument.

title: The Criminal Charges Against Aaron Swartz (legal analysis, parts 1-2)
author: Orin S. Kerr
bias_or_interest: A leading CFAA scholar, not a Swartz partisan. Argues the charges were legally plausible under existing law, but that the stacked statutory maximum wildly overstated his realistic exposure, and criticizes CFAA breadth. High value precisely because he is not an advocate.
relevant_locations: The realistic sentence was a tiny fraction of the theoretical maximum
Critique of overlapping/stacked counts under the CFAA
The headline maximum overstates true exposure
quotation_note: Use Kerr for the 'the 35-year maximum is misleading' analysis; attribute as a named scholar.
notes: The independent legal anchor that lets us state the 35-year figure ONLY as a misleading theoretical maximum.

title: RSS 1.0 (RDF Site Summary) Specification
author: RSS-DEV Working Group (contributors include Aaron Swartz)
bias_or_interest: The specification document itself. Authoritative that Swartz was a listed contributor/co-author of RSS 1.0 — NOT the inventor of RSS.
relevant_locations: Authors/contributors list includes Aaron Swartz
RSS 1.0 published December 2000, when Swartz was a teenager (born 1986)
quotation_note: Say 'co-authored the RSS 1.0 specification as a teenager,' never 'invented RSS.'
notes: Primary anchor for the RSS wording lock.

title: Markdown (project page and Markdown.pl acknowledgements)
author: John Gruber
bias_or_interest: Markdown's creator credits Aaron Swartz as a key collaborator/contributor (e.g., on the original Markdown.pl and design). Authoritative that Swartz was a KEY COLLABORATOR, not the co-creator.
relevant_locations: Gruber credits Swartz's collaboration on Markdown
Swartz built an early Markdown-to-HTML implementation (html2text lineage)
quotation_note: Say 'a key collaborator on Markdown,' never 'co-created Markdown.'
notes: Primary anchor for the Markdown wording lock.

title: JSTOR's statement on the death of Aaron Swartz / Misuse incidents and JSTOR's position
bias_or_interest: The alleged victim's own statement. Authoritative that JSTOR reached a civil settlement, recovered the content, and did NOT seek or want criminal charges. Self-interested in distancing itself from the prosecution, but directly on point.
relevant_locations: JSTOR settled the matter civilly with Swartz
JSTOR did not seek and was not interested in criminal prosecution
Content was secured/returned
quotation_note: State plainly: the 'victim' JSTOR settled civilly and did not want charges. Attribute to JSTOR.
notes: Core to the 'a crime the victim didn't want prosecuted' beat.

title: Aaron's Law Act of 2013 (H.R. 2454, 113th Congress) — bill status
organization: U.S. Congress (sponsors Rep. Zoe Lofgren and Sen. Ron Wyden)
bias_or_interest: Official legislative record. Authoritative that 'Aaron's Law' was introduced to narrow the CFAA and that it did NOT become law.
relevant_locations: Aaron's Law introduced 2013 (and reintroduced later)
Bill did not pass; the CFAA was not meaningfully reformed
quotation_note: State that the bill was proposed and never passed; the CFAA remained substantially unchanged.
notes: Anchor for the 'the law did not change' payoff. Confirm latest status of any reintroduction before publish.

title: The Internet's Own Boy: The Story of Aaron Swartz (documentary film)
author: Brian Knappenberger (director)
bias_or_interest: The definitive narrative documentary, sympathetic to Swartz. Excellent for human/biographical color and on-record interviews; verify load-bearing facts against primary records. Title of this episode is borrowed from it.
relevant_locations: Biography, RSS at a young age, the believer in open knowledge
Demand Progress and the SOPA/PIPA fight
Attorney Elliot Peters on the plea posture (~6 months) vs the trial threat
quotation_note: Narrative/biographical color only; do not quote at length; verify legal facts against primary sources.
rights_note: Copyrighted film (CC-BY-NC-SA release exists). Ideas/facts only; no extended clips.
notes: Human texture and the source for the attorney's account of the plea offer; cross-check Peters' figures before publish.

title: Demand Progress and the SOPA/PIPA blackout (contemporaneous reporting)
organization: Multiple mainstream outlets (e.g., The New York Times technology coverage, January 2012)
bias_or_interest: Mainstream reporting on the January 2012 online protest that stalled SOPA/PIPA. Authoritative that the bills were shelved after the protest; used to support 'a central organizer' framing without overstating sole credit.
relevant_locations: January 18, 2012 web blackout/protest
SOPA and PIPA shelved in Congress shortly after
quotation_note: Swartz/Demand Progress = 'a central organizer' of a broad coalition, not the sole cause. The campaign 'helped defeat' the bills.
notes: Confirms the SOPA/PIPA outcome; keep credit shared across a coalition.

title: Creative Commons — history and technical metadata work
bias_or_interest: Creative Commons' own history. Supports that a teenage Swartz helped build the technical/metadata layer (RDF) of the early licenses. Organization-authored; corroborate with the documentary record.
relevant_locations: Early Creative Commons technical/metadata implementation
Swartz among the young technical contributors
quotation_note: Say 'helped build the technical layer of Creative Commons,' not 'founded' it.
notes: Anchor for the Creative Commons wording lock.

title: Aaron Swartz (encyclopedic overview, for timeline and figure cross-check)
bias_or_interest: Neutral aggregator. Used only to cross-check dates, the Infogami/Reddit merger detail, and the ~4.8M / ~80% figures against the primary sources above.
relevant_locations: Born November 8, 1986; died January 11, 2013, at age 26
Infogami merged with Reddit (2006); 'co-founder' label disputed
~4.8 million articles, ~80% of JSTOR (cross-check)
quotation_note: Cross-check only; never the sole basis for a load-bearing claim.
notes: Timeline and the Reddit 'co-owner via merger' nuance; corroborated by SRC-0011.
